title: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: Kevin G. Volpp, George Loewenstein, David A. Asch

items.subtopic: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics
items.title: Behavioral Economics in Addressing Lifestyle-Related Health Issues
items.content: Lifestyle-related behaviors such as tobacco use, physical inactivity, unhealthy diet, and excessive alcohol consumption contribute to 40% of premature mortality in the U.S. These behaviors also significantly impact conditions like cancer, heart disease, type 2 diabetes, and obesity, which account for approximately 75% of $4.3 trillion spent on health care annually.
keyPoints: Lifestyle-related behaviors underlie 40% of premature mortality in the United States.
Approximately 75% of $4.3 trillion spent on U.S. healthcare is attributable to cancer, heart disease, type 2 diabetes, and obesity.
Behavioral economics can help bridge the gap between theoretical health goals and actual outcomes by considering human behavior complexities.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics

items.subtopic: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics
items.title: Challenges in Medication Adherence Post-Myocardial Infarction
items.content: Despite being prescribed medications to lower cholesterol after a myocardial infarction, nearly one-half of patients discontinue these drugs within a year, even when provided free. This highlights the gap between health care advancements and patient behavior, influenced by factors like limited attention and self-control issues.
keyPoints: Nearly 50% of patients stop taking cholesterol-lowering medications within a year post-myocardial infarction.
Medication adherence is affected by human behavioral factors such as limited attention and problems with self-control.
Designing interventions based on actual human decision-making processes, rather than rational models, can improve health outcomes.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)
items.title: Rational Utility Maximization in Classical Economics
items.content: Classical economics assumes individuals are rational utility maximizers, making decisions based on calculated probabilities and outcomes. This assumption limits policy interventions to cases of externalities, market failures, or information asymmetries.
keyPoints: Individuals are assumed to be rational utility maximizers.
Policy interventions justified only in cases of externalities, market failures, or information asymmetries.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)
items.title: Introduction of Internalities in Behavioral Economics
items.content: Behavioral economics expands on classical theories by introducing 'internalities,' where individuals impose costs on their future selves, justifying policy interventions to protect against self-inflicted harm.
keyPoints: 'Internalities' refer to costs individuals impose on themselves, particularly their future selves.
Policy interventions can be justified to address internalities and protect individuals from their own irrational decisions.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)
items.title: Libertarian Paternalism and Nudges
items.content: Behavioral economics introduces 'libertarian paternalism' and 'asymmetric paternalism,' which protect individuals from irrational decisions without restricting freedom, using strategies like nudges.
keyPoints: 'Libertarian paternalism' aims to guide choices while preserving freedom.
'Asymmetric paternalism' helps those prone to irrational decisions without limiting informed choices.
Nudges are subtle interventions that encourage beneficial behaviors.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 2)
items.title: Understanding Nudges in Behavioral Economics
items.content: Nudges are subtle changes to the choice architecture that influence behavior without restricting options or significantly altering economic incentives. They must be easy and inexpensive to avoid.
keyPoints: A nudge alters behavior predictably without forbidding options or changing economic incentives significantly.
Examples include defaults in retirement savings plans and automatic escalation for higher savings rates.
Nudges have impacted global retirement savings policies.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 2)
items.title: The Role of Incentives in Behavioral Economics
items.content: Behavioral economics distinguishes itself from traditional economics by focusing on how incentives are structured and delivered, rather than just their magnitude.
keyPoints: Traditional economic incentives involve straightforward financial transactions like wages or fines.
Behavioral economics explores delivering incentives in ways that maximize effectiveness, even with small investments.
Internalities highlight situations where traditional economics might not suggest incentives, but behavioral approaches do.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 2)

items.subtopic: USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 1)
items.title: Leveraging Loss Aversion in Healthcare Incentives
items.content: Behavioral economics, particularly through the concept of loss aversion, can be utilized to design more effective healthcare incentives. Physicians are more motivated by penalties than equivalent rewards due to their inherent dislike for losses.
keyPoints: Loss aversion ratio ranges from 1.5 to 2.5, indicating that a penalty is perceived as more motivating than an equivalently sized reward.
Incentives framed as potential losses (e.g., missing out on $1000) are more effective motivators compared to equivalent gains ($1500-$2500).
Understanding this can optimize the design of provider payment systems and other health-related incentive programs.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 1)

items.subtopic: USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 1)
items.title: Impact of Framing on Decision-Making in Healthcare
items.content: The way information is framed significantly influences decision-making among patients and physicians. Presenting outcomes in terms of gains rather than losses can lead to different choices, even if the statistical probabilities are equivalent.
keyPoints: Framing effects show that people prefer options presented with positive outcomes (e.g., survival rates) over those with negative outcomes (e.g., death rates).
A 68% chance of survival is more compelling than a 32% chance of dying, despite being statistically equivalent.
Clinicians can use framing strategically to guide patient decisions while maintaining ethical standards.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 1)

items.subtopic: USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 1)
items.title: Ethical Considerations in Using Behavioral Economics
items.content: While behavioral economics can guide patients towards beneficial health decisions, it raises ethical questions about the balance between guidance and paternalism.
keyPoints: Clinicians must carefully frame information to avoid biasing patient choices unduly.
Balancing truthful communication with strategic framing is crucial to maintain trust and autonomy.
The role of clinicians may extend from neutral informants to trusted advisors, justifying the use of behavioral economics in certain contexts.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 1)

items.subtopic: USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 2)
items.title: Leveraging Loss Aversion and Overoptimism in Health Behavior Change
items.content: Utilize the concepts of loss aversion and overoptimism to design effective health behavior change programs. People often exhibit overoptimism about their ability to maintain self-control, such as adhering to gym routines or weight loss plans. By framing potential outcomes as losses rather than gains, individuals may be more motivated to achieve their health goals.
keyPoints: Overoptimism can lead to unrealistic expectations about self-control (e.g., 'This time my efforts at weight loss will succeed!').
Loss aversion is more motivating than equivalent gains for meeting health behavior goals.
Deposit contracts leverage overoptimism and loss aversion by having individuals risk their own money to meet health-behavior goals.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 2)

items.subtopic: USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 2)
items.title: Understanding the Peanuts Effect in Health Behavior Interventions
items.content: The peanuts effect highlights that small rewards may be less motivating than larger ones, challenging strategies focused on incremental changes. This concept can explain why individuals might rationalize harmful behaviors as inconsequential when viewed individually.
keyPoints: Diminishing marginal utility implies smaller gains and losses are disproportionately motivating compared to larger ones.
Small rewards (e.g., two $5 rewards) may be less effective than a single larger reward (one $10 reward).
Encouraging small, frequent health investments can be beneficial due to the tendency to underweight small outcomes.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 2)

items.subtopic: USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 2)
items.title: Optimizing Incentive Programs Using Present-Biased Preferences
items.content: Design incentive programs that provide frequent feedback to align with present-biased preferences, while aggregating financial rewards into substantial payments. This approach leverages the peanuts effect by making small periodic investments more appealing.
keyPoints: Frequent (often daily) feedback on rewards can enhance motivation due to present-biased preferences.
Aggregating financial rewards into larger monthly payments makes them more attention-worthy.
Encourage small, periodic health investments like medication adherence or retirement savings.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 2)

items.explanation: All listed behaviors are estimated to contribute to premature mortality.

items.question: What percentage of health care spending in the United States is attributable to cancer, heart disease, type 2 diabetes, and obesity?
items.explanation: Approximately 75% of health care spending is due to these conditions.

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)
items.question: According to classical economic theory, what is the primary assumption about individual decision-making?
options: Individuals make decisions based on emotional responses.
Individuals rely heavily on external advice for making decisions.
Individuals prioritize short-term benefits over long-term consequences.
items.explanation: Classical economics assumes individuals are rational and make decisions to maximize their net present value.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)
items.question: Under classical economic theory, when is government intervention justified?
options: In the presence of externalities or market failures.
To promote individual freedom of choice.
Whenever there are health-related behaviors involved.
items.explanation: Government interventions are justified in classical economics when dealing with externalities, market failures, or information asymmetries.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)
items.question: What does behavioral economics introduce to justify policy interventions?
options: The principle of rational utility maximization.
items.explanation: Behavioral economics introduces the notion of 'internalities' to justify policy interventions, addressing costs individuals impose on themselves.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary goal of asymmetric paternalism in behavioral economics?
options: To restrict freedom of choice for all individuals.
To protect those prone to irrational decisions without limiting informed choices.
To eliminate all forms of market failures.
To ensure that only rational decisions are made.
items.explanation: Asymmetric paternalism aims to help those making irrational decisions while preserving freedom for those making informed, deliberate choices.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following best describes a 'nudge' as defined in behavioral economics?
options: An intervention that alters people's behavior by forbidding certain options.
A change in economic incentives to significantly alter choice architecture.
Any aspect of choice architecture that predictably alters behavior without forbidding options or changing economic incentives significantly.
A financial incentive designed to promote behavior change.
items.explanation: A nudge is defined as altering behavior in a predictable way without forbidding any options or significantly changing economic incentives.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is NOT an example of a nudge as discussed in the passage?
options: Defaults to increase enrollment in retirement savings plans.
Automatic escalation to encourage higher savings rates.
Incentive programs aimed at changing health behaviors for cost-effectiveness.
Improvements in the delivery of health-related information.
items.explanation: Incentive programs aimed at changing health behaviors do not fit the definition of a nudge as they involve financial incentives, which are part of traditional economics.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 2)

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 2)
items.question: According to the passage, what is a common misconception about behavioral economics?
options: It only applies to health-related policies.
It involves deploying financial incentives for behavior change.
items.explanation: The misconception is that using financial incentives equates to behavioral economics, whereas it actually reflects traditional economics.
items.reference: Improving Health and Health Care Using Behavioral Economics · CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 2)

items.subtopic: USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 1)
items.question: Which concept in prospect theory suggests that people evaluate outcomes as gains or losses based on their starting point?
items.explanation: Reference-dependence is the concept where decision-makers evaluate outcomes as gains or losses depending on their starting point.

items.subtopic: USING BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS TO PROMOTE SELF-BENEFICIAL HEALTH BEHAVIORS (part 1)
items.question: According to prospect theory, how do people generally react to losses compared to equivalent-sized gains?
options: They are indifferent between the two
They dislike losses more than they like equivalent-sized gains
They like losses more than equivalent-sized gains
items.explanation: Loss aversion implies that people dislike losses much more than they like equivalent-sized gains.

Radiation therapy is preferred when outcomes are framed in terms of death probability
Framing has no effect on the choice
Both treatments are equally preferred regardless of framing
items.explanation: The passage indicates that surgery was more popular when its outcomes were framed in terms of survival probability.

items.question: What behavioral economic principle suggests that framing reward outcomes in terms of losses can be more motivating than equivalent gains?
items.explanation: Loss aversion suggests that framing outcomes in terms of losses can be more motivating than equivalent gains.

items.question: What is the term for the phenomenon where small rewards are less motivating when presented separately compared to a larger single reward?

As a result of present-biased preferences

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)
items.assertion: Behavioral economics broadens the range of policy interventions compared to classical economics.

items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why behavioral economics offers a broader range of interventions.

items.subtopic: CONCEPTS OF CLASSICAL ECONOMICS AND HOW THEY DIFFER FROM BEHAVIORAL ECONOMICS (part 1)
items.assertion: Classical economics justifies government intervention primarily to promote individual freedom.
items.reason: It focuses on addressing irrational behaviors and internalities.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; classical economics does not focus on promoting individual freedom but rather intervenes in cases of externalities or market failures. The reason is also false as it pertains to behavioral, not classical, economics.

items.reason: Small rewards can be more motivating when aggregated into larger sums.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, as the peanuts effect suggests that small rewards may not always be motivating unless aggregated.
